title: Production Data Stack
body: When you read the daily report in isolation, a bad utilization number tells you *what* happened but not *why* or *where to look*. Investigations stall, fixes get aimed at the wrong unit or shift, and the same problem shows up the next day.
keyPoints: The four workbooks form a stack — running-hours log → A&U workbook → daily report → INX-SUM survey — and each layer answers a question the one above can't. Never pull one workbook without knowing where it sits in the chain.

When the daily report flags a bad number, read *down*: daily report tells you the number, A&U names the unit and reason, running-hours log gives you raw hours plus the operator's Emp. No, INX-SUM checks whether the ground actually moved. Going up the stack narrows cause; going down it confirms it.
Diesel burn out of ratio with running hours in the running-hours log is an early fault signal — it appears before the A&U workbook logs a breakdown reason. Spot it there and you've got time to act.

A high truck count against a low INX-SUM survey number doesn't mean the fleet underperformed — it usually means loading position fell outside the survey calculation polygon. Check before you conclude.
apply: North Dump opens today (Saturday Jun 27) — if truck counts look strong but the INX-SUM daily pickup comes in low, loading position at the dump rather than fleet performance is the first place to check.
